name: cli-tool
label: CLI Tool
description: Command-line tool projects: argument parsing, terminal UX, shell integration, cross-platform distribution.
profile_description: Runs locally on user machines. Prioritize fast startup, clear error messages, and zero-surprise defaults.
run_context_additions: - Every command must exit with a meaningful exit code: 0 for success, non-zero for failure.
- Error messages go to stderr; all normal output goes to stdout.
- Never use interactive prompts without a non-interactive fallback flag.
- Test on macOS, Linux, and Windows (or document explicitly unsupported platforms).
- Keep startup time under 200ms for simple commands — avoid heavy imports at module load.
workflow_notes: Design the help text first: if `--help` is confusing, the UX is broken.
Follow POSIX conventions: short flags (-v) and long flags (--verbose) for all options.
Provide machine-readable output mode (--json or --output json) for scripting consumers.
Use explicit defaults and document them in --help; never rely on ambient env vars silently.

Config discipline: collect tunable defaults into one config module wired through argparse (env-overridable, resolved at call time); classify before centralizing — some values stay coupled or frozen deliberately, record why — and guard the convention with a tripwire test.
Write integration tests that invoke the CLI as a subprocess to catch argument parsing bugs.
safety_notes: Never delete files without --dry-run showing what would be removed first.
Destructive operations (delete, overwrite, reset) require explicit confirmation or --force.
Validate all path arguments before acting — reject traversal patterns (../) explicitly.
Credentials passed via flags are visible in process lists; prefer env vars or prompt input.
Guard against partial writes: write to a temp file and rename atomically where possible.
